name: Text, images, video — why can some AIs see and make things?
tagline: You've seen AI write an essay, paint a picture, and generate a video clip, and you're not sure if that's one technology or three. It's one. It's the same trick — learn patterns from millions of examples, then make more of the same material — pointed at different materials. Learn that idea and the whole confusing zoo of AI tools falls into place.
overview: The module that kills the question "some AIs write, some paint, some film — are they even the same thing?" They are, and the answer is genuinely simple. Modern AI does one thing: it studies an enormous pile of examples, learns the patterns in them, and then generates more material that fits those patterns. Feed it text and it learns to write; feed it images and it learns to paint; feed it video and it learns to make clips. Same family, different diet. From there, two directions a beginner should keep separate. UNDERSTANDING is when you show the AI something — a photo, a document, a chart — and it tells you what's in it; that's "reading" more than words. MAKING is when you describe what you want and it generates new text, images, audio, or video. An AI that can juggle several of these kinds of material at once — read a photo, listen to audio, answer in text — is called multimodal, and once you know the word means "several modes," you can forget it. The module closes on honest expectations, because they matter: text is the most mature and dependable, images are strong, video is the newest and improving fast — and everything from the first module still holds. Beautiful output is not the same as true output; a generated picture or clip is a draft you review before it ever represents you, because hallucination has visual forms too. Nothing here is technical. It's a map, and one idea that makes the map readable.
objectives: Explain in one plain sentence why text AI, image AI, and video AI are the same idea and not three separate technologies — each one learned patterns from millions of examples of its material, then generates more of that material.

Keep two directions separate: understanding (you show it something and it tells you what's in it) and making (you describe something and it generates new material), and give an everyday example of each.

Say what "multimodal" means in plain words — an AI that handles several kinds of material at once — and set honest expectations: text is most dependable, images are strong, video is newest, and anything generated still gets a human look before it represents you.

lessons.title: One trick, different materials: why writing, painting, and filming AIs are the same idea
lessons.objective: Understand that text, image, and video AIs are one family running the same learn-patterns-then-generate trick on different materials, and keep the two directions — understanding versus making — clearly apart.
lessons.body:
## The confusion, out loud

You've watched an AI write a decent essay. You've seen one turn a sentence into a painting. You've seen another spin up a short video clip from a description. And a fair question sits underneath all of it: *is that one technology doing three tricks, or three completely different technologies that happen to share the word "AI"?*

It's one. And the reason is worth saying slowly, because once it clicks, the whole confusing zoo of AI tools quietly organises itself.

## The one trick

Here is the single idea this whole module rests on. Every one of these AIs does the *same thing*: it is shown an **enormous pile of examples**, it **learns the patterns** in them, and then it **generates new material that fits those patterns**. That's it. Learn from a mountain of examples, then make more of the same kind.

Now just change what's in the pile.

- Show it a mountain of **text** — books, articles, conversations — and the patterns it learns are how words follow words. Point it at a blank page and it *writes*.
- Show it a mountain of **images** paired with descriptions and it learns how the word "lighthouse" tends to correspond to certain shapes, colours, and arrangements of pixels. Ask for a lighthouse at dusk and it *paints* one.
- Show it a mountain of **video** and it learns how images tend to change from one frame to the next — how things move. Describe a scene and it *makes a clip*.

Same trick every time. The AIs feel different because their *diet* was different, not because the underlying idea changed. A helpful way to hold it: **same family, different diet.** If it learned from a kind of material, it can make more of that kind of material.

And notice what this rules out. The image AI is **not** flipping through a giant photo album and handing you a picture it found. It's generating a brand-new one from the patterns it learned — the same way the text AI writes a sentence nobody wrote before. "It looks things up" is the wrong picture. "It learned the patterns and generates fresh" is the right one — and it's exactly the next-word-prediction idea from the first module, just aimed at pixels and frames instead of words.

## Two directions to keep apart: understanding vs making

With the one trick in hand, there's a second distinction that saves a lot of muddle. AI can go in **two directions** with a kind of material, and beginners tend to blur them.

**Understanding** is the *input* direction: you **show it something**, and it tells you what's in it. You photograph the bookshelf in your office and ask *"which of these is worth reading first?"* — and it reads the spines, recognises the titles, and answers. You hand it a two-page contract and ask *"what am I actually agreeing to here?"* You give it a screenshot of a confusing error and ask what it means. In every case you supplied the material; the AI's job was to *make sense of it*. This is the part that feels almost magical the first time — the AI "reading" something that isn't words.

**Making** is the *output* direction: you **describe what you want**, and it generates brand-new material. *"Draw me a poster for a Saturday bake sale, warm and friendly."* *"Write a two-line caption for this."* *"Turn this idea into a short video."* You brought the description; the AI brought the material.

One everyday pair to lock it in. **Understanding:** photograph your bookshelf and ask what's worth reading — you gave it the picture, it gave you the answer. **Making:** describe a poster and get one drawn — you gave it the words, it gave you the picture. Same technology, opposite directions. Whenever an AI tool confuses you, the first question that cuts through is simply: *is this reading something I gave it, or making something new for me?*

## The word for handling several materials at once: multimodal

Here's the last piece, and it's just a vocabulary word so you stop being thrown by it.

Some AIs are happy with only one kind of material — text in, text out. But many modern ones can juggle **several kinds at once**: you can show one a photo *and* type a question about it *and* get a spoken answer back. The plain word for "a kind of material" — text, images, audio, video — is a **mode**. So an AI that works across several of them is **multimodal**: *multi* (several) *modal* (modes). Several kinds of material, one AI.

That's the whole definition. It sounds impressive in a headline, but now you can hear it and translate instantly: *"oh — it just means this one can handle more than plain text."* You can put the word down and keep the idea.

So the map, in one breath: **one trick — learn patterns, then generate — pointed at different materials (text, images, audio, video); two directions (understand what you show it, or make something new); and "multimodal" for the AIs that do several materials at once.** That's the entire territory. The next lesson is about being honest about how well each part actually works — because that's where people get burned.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Why are a text-writing AI, an image-painting AI, and a video-making AI considered the same idea rather than three unrelated technologies?

options.text: Each one learned the patterns in a huge pile of examples of its own material, then generates more of that material — same trick, different diet
They all secretly search a giant library and hand you the closest text, picture, or clip they find already stored
They only look like different tools; underneath, a single program does writing, painting, and filming in one fixed way
They share the word "AI" for marketing reasons but actually work on completely different principles

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The unifying idea is one trick — learn patterns from millions of examples, then generate new material that fits — applied to different materials: fed text it writes, fed images it paints, fed video it makes clips. It is not looking things up in a stored library and returning a match (b); it generates something fresh, the same way the text AI writes a sentence nobody wrote before. It also isn't a single monolithic program (c) — these are often separate tools with the same underlying approach, which is exactly why "same family, different diet" fits. And they share more than a marketing label (d): they genuinely share the learn-then-generate principle.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You photograph a two-page contract and ask the AI "what am I actually agreeing to here?" Which direction is this, and why?

options.text: Understanding — you showed it material you already have and asked it to make sense of what's in it
Making — because it produces a written answer, so it is generating new material from your description
Neither — reading a document isn't something AI can do, only writing new text is
Both are identical, so the direction doesn't matter and isn't worth separating

knowledgeCheck.explanation: You supplied the material — the photographed contract — and asked the AI to tell you what's in it; that's the understanding (input) direction, "reading" something you provided. It isn't making (b): making is when you describe what you want and it generates brand-new material like a poster or a clip — here you didn't ask it to invent a contract, you asked it to read yours. AI genuinely can take in images and documents, so "it can't read" is wrong (c). And the two directions are worth keeping apart (d) — asking "is this reading what I gave it, or making something new?" is the quickest way to understand any AI tool that confuses you.

lessons.title: How good is each one, really — and why you still look before it goes out
lessons.objective: Set honest, durable expectations across text, images, and video, recognise that hallucination has visual forms, and understand why generated material is a draft to review — closing with how this shows up in Automatos.
lessons.body:
## Same idea, different maturity

The last lesson said text, image, and video AI are all the same trick. True — but that does **not** mean they all work equally well today. Being honest about this is what keeps you from being disappointed, or worse, embarrassed.

A fair picture, without naming any product as "the best":

- **Text is the most mature and dependable.** It's had the most attention for the longest, and for everyday reading and writing it's genuinely reliable. This is the part you can lean on hardest.
- **Images are strong.** You can get a striking, usable picture from a sentence, and it's good enough for real work. It still slips on fine detail — the small print, the exact number of fingers on a hand, the precise wording on a sign it draws.
- **Video is the newest and improving fastest.** Think short clips, not finished films, and expect it to change quickly. It's the frontier, which means it's the most impressive to watch and the least dependable to rely on.

Hold this loosely on purpose. The *order* — text steadiest, images strong, video newest — is the durable part worth remembering. The exact capabilities move month to month, so don't anchor to a specific claim about what any tool can or can't do right now.

## Hallucination has visual forms too

Here's the trap, and it catches careful people. In the first module you met **hallucination** — an AI stating something false with total confidence, because it produces what *fits the pattern* rather than what it has verified. That property doesn't disappear when the material stops being text. It just puts on a costume.

In a generated **image**, hallucination looks like: a hand with six fingers, a company logo that's subtly wrong, text on a sign that's garbled nonsense, a reflection that couldn't physically exist, an invented detail that was never in your brief. In a generated **video**, it's objects that warp or flicker between frames, or physics that quietly breaks. None of this is the tool malfunctioning — it's the *exact same behaviour* as the confidently-wrong sentence: the AI generated something plausible-looking from patterns, with no separate sense of "but is this actually right?"

And there's a sharper edge on the visual version: a polished image *looks* authoritative in a way a paragraph doesn't. Fluent writing already fools people; a crisp, professional-looking picture fools them faster. **Beautiful is not the same as true** — arguably even less so once it's a picture, because the polish does extra work to lower your guard.

## So: generated material is a draft you review

Which lands us exactly where the first module did, and it's the rule that ties this whole track together. **Anything the AI generates — a paragraph, a picture, a clip — is a draft, not a finished fact.** Before it represents you, a person looks at it. Not out of fear of the technology; as ordinary quality control, the same glance you'd give an intern's work before it went to a client.

Concretely: the AI drafts the bake-sale poster in seconds and it's genuinely good — but you check the date on it is right and the shop name is spelled correctly before you print two hundred copies. The one-idea version to carry out of here: **pick the tool by the material your job needs — writing, an image, a clip — and give whatever it generates a human look before it goes out.**

## The Automatos peek

This "same trick, different materials" idea is exactly why, in Automatos, you can hand your workspace more than plain text. Its knowledge tools are documented as **multimodal** — the Documents area handles not just text and Office files but PDFs with embedded graphics, scanned pages read via text recognition, and other mixed-content material. So the things you upload for the AI to draw on aren't limited to words on a page; the same principle you just learned — if it learned from a kind of material, it can read it — is what lets one workspace make sense of the mixed reality of a real business, where the useful information lives in scans, screenshots, and documents as much as in tidy paragraphs.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Which honest summary of today's maturity — the durable part worth remembering — is right?

options.text: Text is the most mature and dependable, images are strong, and video is the newest and improving fastest
Video is the most reliable of the three because it's the most impressive to watch
They're all the same trick, so they must all work equally well right now
None of them is usable for real work yet, so it's safest to avoid all three

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The durable ordering is text steadiest, images strong, video newest and moving fastest — that order is the part to remember, while exact capabilities shift month to month. Impressiveness isn't reliability (b): video is the frontier, the most dazzling to watch and the least dependable to lean on. Being the same underlying trick doesn't make them equally mature (c) — same idea, different amounts of time and refinement. And they're plainly usable for real work (d): text is dependable and images are strong enough to use, with a review.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A generated product image looks crisp and professional, but the logo on the box is subtly wrong and a sign in the background reads as garbled letters. What is this, and what should you do?

options.text: It's hallucination in visual form — the AI generated plausible-looking detail it never verified; treat the image as a draft and give it a human look before it represents you
The image tool is broken and can't be trusted for anything again
Because the image looks polished and professional, it must be accurate and needs no review
Hallucination only happens in text, so a wrong logo in an image must be something else entirely

knowledgeCheck.explanation: A wrong logo and garbled sign text are hallucination wearing a visual costume — the same confidently-wrong behaviour as an invented sentence, because the AI generated what fit the pattern without checking it, so the fix is the same: treat it as a draft and review it. It isn't a broken tool (b) — this is normal behaviour for how generation works. Polish is not proof (c); a crisp picture can fool you faster than a paragraph precisely because it looks authoritative. And hallucination is not text-only (d) — it shows up as extra fingers, wrong logos, garbled signs, and warping video too.

questions.stem: What is the single idea that explains why text, image, and video AIs are one family?

options.text: Each learned patterns from a huge pile of examples of its material, then generates more of that material — one trick, different diets
Each stores a copy of the whole internet and hands back the closest match it can find
Each has genuine understanding of the world and creates from real knowledge
Each is powered by a different, unrelated breakthrough that happens to be called AI

questions.explanation: The one idea is learn-patterns-then-generate, pointed at different materials — text makes writing, images make pictures, video makes clips. It doesn't store and return matches (b): it generates fresh material from learned patterns, the way it writes a never-before-seen sentence. It isn't understanding the world from real knowledge (c) — it's pattern completion, which is why it can be confidently wrong. And they're not unrelated breakthroughs (d); they share the very same principle, just fed different material.

questions.stem: What does "multimodal" mean, in plain words?

options.text: An AI that can handle several kinds of material at once — for example read a photo, take a typed question, and answer in text
An AI that is faster and more powerful than a normal one
An AI that can only work with one kind of material at a time
A special premium mode you switch on to unlock better answers

questions.explanation: A "mode" is a kind of material — text, images, audio, video — so multimodal means several modes: one AI that works across more than one kind of material at once. It's not a claim about raw speed or power (b). It's the opposite of single-material (c) — that would be handling only one kind. And it isn't a premium toggle (d); it simply describes the range of materials an AI can take in and produce.

questions.stem: You describe a scene in words and the AI generates a short video clip of it. Which direction is that?

options.text: Making — you supplied a description and it generated brand-new material
Understanding — because it had to understand your words first
Neither — generating video isn't something AI can attempt
Both at once, which is why the two directions can't be told apart

questions.explanation: You brought a description and it produced new material — that's the making (output) direction. The label follows what the AI produced, not the fact that it parsed your words along the way, so calling it understanding misses the point (b): understanding is when you show it existing material and it tells you what's in it. AI genuinely can generate short clips (c) — that's video generation, the newest of the three. And the directions are distinct and useful to separate (d): here you clearly asked it to make something new, not to read something you gave it.

questions.stem: When an image AI produces a picture of a lighthouse, what is actually happening?

options.text: It generates a new image from the patterns it learned linking the idea of a lighthouse to shapes and colours — it isn't fetching a stored photo
It searches a photo library and returns the best real lighthouse photo it finds
It copies an existing image and lightly edits it to look new
It looks up a definition of "lighthouse" and renders that definition exactly

questions.explanation: It generates a fresh image from learned patterns — the same generate-don't-retrieve behaviour as the text AI writing a new sentence. It isn't searching a library and returning a found photo (b), which is the most common misconception this module corrects. It isn't copying and lightly editing one existing image (c) — it's producing something new from patterns across millions of examples. And it isn't rendering a dictionary definition (d); there's no literal definition being drawn, just patterns turned into pixels.

questions.stem: Which statement about how good these AIs are today is both honest and durable — unlikely to go out of date?

options.text: As a rough order, text is the most dependable, images are strong, and video is newest and improving fastest — while exact capabilities keep changing
Video AI has now surpassed text AI in reliability for everyday work
Image AI is flawless and its output never needs checking
A specific product is the definitive best at all three and will stay that way

questions.explanation: The durable claim is the relative order plus the caveat that specifics move — text steadiest, images strong, video newest and fastest-changing. A firm reliability ranking of video over text (b) is both currently backwards and the kind of dated claim to avoid. "Flawless, never check" (c) contradicts the whole review discipline — images still slip on fine detail. And crowning one product as permanently best (d) is exactly the sort of statement that rots, which this track deliberately refuses to make.

questions.stem: A teammate says: "The image came out looking really professional, so we can skip the review and publish it." What's the problem with that reasoning?

options.text: Polish isn't accuracy — hallucination shows up in images too (wrong logos, garbled text, impossible details), and a crisp picture can fool you faster, so it still gets a human look
There's no problem — a professional-looking image is reliable evidence that every detail is correct
The only issue is that images always take too long to review to be worth it
Images can't contain mistakes, so the real risk is only ever in generated text

questions.explanation: Beautiful is not true: a generated image can carry confident errors — a subtly wrong logo, garbled sign text, an impossible reflection — and its polish actively lowers your guard, so it earns a review, not a pass. Professional appearance is not proof of correctness (b); that's the exact trap. Review isn't skipped for being slow (c) — a quick glance is cheap next to publishing a visible mistake. And images absolutely can contain mistakes (d) — hallucination isn't text-only.

questions.stem: You hold up your phone camera to a shelf of products and ask the AI "which of these is low stock and needs reordering?" Setting aside whether it has your live data, which capability of the AI does the reading-the-photo part rely on?

options.text: Understanding — showing it an image and having it make sense of what's in the picture
Making — because it generates a helpful answer at the end
Multimodal is a myth; AI can only ever read text you type
It must be searching the internet for a matching photo of your shelf

questions.explanation: Taking in a photo and interpreting what's in it is the understanding direction — "reading" an image rather than words, which is what makes multimodal AI feel striking. The making label (b) fixes on the wrong step: producing a sentence at the end doesn't change that the core skill here is reading the picture you supplied. Reading images is real, not a myth (c) — that's exactly what a multimodal AI does. And it isn't finding your shelf online (d); it's interpreting the specific image you gave it.

questions.stem: What's the most useful first question to ask yourself when a new AI tool confuses you?

options.text: "Is this reading something I gave it, or making something new for me?" — understanding versus making
"Which brand made it?" — since the brand decides everything about what it can do
"How large is its training data?" — the only number that matters
"Is it conscious?" — because that determines whether it's safe to use

questions.explanation: Sorting a tool into understanding (it reads what you supply) versus making (it generates new material) is the fastest way to grasp what it's actually for. The brand isn't the deciding factor (b) — different products run the same underlying trick, a point this whole track leans on. A raw training-data figure isn't a practical guide for a beginner (c) and isn't "the only number that matters." And consciousness is a category error (d): these systems aren't conscious, and that's not the useful question — what you gave it versus what it makes is.

resources.title: Automatos AI — Documents (multimodal knowledge, in its own words)
resources.annotation: The platform's own document area, and the grounding for this module's peek. It lists the material you can upload — text and Office files, plus PDFs including scanned documents read via text recognition — and has a dedicated Multimodal section for rich-media documents: images with extracted text, PDFs with embedded graphics, mixed-content files. Concrete proof of the module's point that "if it learned from a kind of material, it can read it" applies to a real workspace, not just plain paragraphs.

resources.title: Google — Generative AI Leader exam guide (Fundamentals of generative AI)
resources.annotation: A vendor-light, business-toned primer on what generative AI is and how it behaves. For this module, read the "Fundamentals of gen AI" material: it backs the one-trick idea — a system that learns from examples and generates new content that fits the patterns — across text and other materials, and supports the honest note that generated output is plausible, not guaranteed true. Beginner-friendly and non-technical.

resources.title: Hugging Face — "What is" multimodal / task explainer pages
resources.annotation: A maker's plain catalogue of AI tasks by the kind of material involved — text generation, image generation, image-to-text, text-to-video, and more. Useful here purely as a map: it makes visible that the same generate-from-patterns idea is applied task by task across materials, and that understanding (reading what you supply) and making (generating something new) are genuinely different jobs. Skim for orientation, not depth; ignore the technical model listings.

resources.title: Google — a plain explainer on multimodal AI (several kinds of input at once)
resources.annotation: A general-audience overview of AI that works across several kinds of material — text, images, audio, video — rather than text alone. Used here only to reinforce the plain meaning of "multimodal" (several modes) and the honest framing that different materials are at different stages of maturity. Orientation reading; take no specific capability claim from it as fixed, since those move quickly.

videos.title: 05 · One trick, different materials — how one idea makes writing, image, and video AI in 7 minutes
05 · Deep Dive — understanding vs making, multimodal, and reviewing what AI generates
